title: Applying How Vocabulary is Learned
description: DOK1 Assessment - Applying principles from vocabulary research to educational programming
author: Marcello Sgambelluri
sources: Paul Nation (BNC/COCA), Averil Coxhead (AWL), Stuart Webb, Higa & Nation, Griffin & Harley, Kate Nation, Hu & Nation, E.D. Hirsch
notes: Comprehensive brainlift applying vocabulary acquisition research to 2 Hour Learning model. Focus on frequency-based instruction, productive retrieval over recognition, and 98% coverage threshold.
flags: Fact 2.4 (Score 2): The '50-100%' increase in difficulty from semantic sets is a specific statistical claim that needs a direct source. Interference theory is valid but this number appears unverified.
Missing source link for Kate Nation Lexical Quality Hypothesis (Fact 3.5 notes 'link:' with nothing after it in the brainlift).

facts.fact: The first 1,000 word families cover ~85% of conversational English and ~70% of written text
facts.note: Verified. PMC study confirms 'around 85% of the three corpora were made up of the most frequent word families.' Academic written coverage at 67-68% per SAGE research.

facts.fact: The second 1,000 (2K) adds only ~4-5% coverage
facts.note: Verified. 1K+2K = ~90% total, meaning 2K adds ~5%. Consistent across multiple corpus studies.

facts.fact: Native speakers typically enter school knowing 3,000-5,000 word families orally
facts.note: Partially Verified. Commonly cited figure but specific source not located in verification.

facts.fact: Vocabulary beyond the 2K band drops off rapidly in frequency but increases in specific semantic value
facts.note: Verified. PMC study: 'after the 5,000 level, the figures dropped below 1% for all the corpora.'

facts.fact: The AWL contains 570 word families that appear with high frequency across all academic disciplines
facts.note: Verified. Coxhead (2000) TESOL Quarterly: 'The AWL contains 570 word families.' Wikipedia, EAPFoundation confirm.

facts.fact: These words account for ~10% of the total words in academic texts
facts.note: Verified. Coxhead (2000): '570 word families that account for approximately 10.0% of the total words (tokens) in academic texts.'

facts.fact: They are 'Tier 2' words: high utility but low frequency in casual conversation
facts.note: Verified. Coxhead: AWL covers 'only 1.4% of the total words in a fiction collection' vs 10% in academic.

facts.fact: Incidental learning (learning just by reading) requires 12+ encounters to learn a single word
facts.note: Partially Verified. EAPFoundation: 'Studies have variously suggested 6, 8, 10, 12 or more than 20 encounters may be needed.' Not specifically 12+.

facts.fact: L1 learners typically need to read ~1 million words per year to maintain natural growth rates
facts.note: Partially Verified. Commonly cited Nation figure but specific source not located in verification.

facts.fact: Deliberate learning (flashcards, drills) yields significantly larger gains in shorter timeframes compared to reading alone
facts.note: Mostly Verified. Webb (2008) supports faster gains from deliberate learning, but also advocates for combined approach.

facts.fact: Teaching words in semantic sets (e.g., colors, fruits, opposites) increases learning difficulty by 50-100%
facts.note: Weakly Supported. Interference theory is real, but specific '50-100%' figure not verified in search results.

facts.fact: The brain struggles to distinguish between similar items learned simultaneously (cross-association)
facts.note: Mostly Verified. Consistent with interference theory in vocabulary learning literature.

facts.fact: Interference persists over time if the initial encoding was clustered
facts.note: Mostly Verified. Consistent with interference theory.

facts.fact: Receptive Retrieval (seeing word -> recalling meaning) is easier and decays faster
facts.note: Mostly Verified. Consistent with vocabulary research on receptive vs productive knowledge.

facts.fact: Productive Retrieval (seeing meaning -> producing word) is cognitively harder but leads to stronger retention
facts.note: Verified. Well-established in vocabulary acquisition research.

facts.fact: The 'Testing Effect' shows that the act of retrieval itself strengthens the neural pathway
facts.note: Verified. Testing effect is well-established in cognitive psychology (Roediger & Karpicke, 2006).

facts.fact: High-quality lexical representations include precise orthography (spelling), phonology (sound), and semantics (meaning)
facts.note: Verified. Core of Lexical Quality Hypothesis.

facts.fact: Low-quality representations (knowing the 'gist' but not the spelling) slow down reading processing speeds
facts.note: Mostly Verified. Consistent with LQH but source link missing in brainlift.

facts.fact: L1 speakers often suffer from high semantic knowledge but low orthographic precision
facts.note: Mostly Verified. Consistent with LQH framework.

facts.fact: 98% coverage (1 unknown word in 50) is the threshold for unassisted comprehension of written text
facts.note: Verified. Hu & Nation (2000): '98% of lexical items for adequate text comprehension.' Nation (2006) confirms.

facts.fact: 95% coverage is adequate for spoken discourse but insufficient for academic reading
facts.note: Verified. Laufer (1989): 95% = 'adequate' comprehension. Hu & Nation: 98% needed for 'full, unassisted comprehension.'

facts.fact: At 90% coverage (1 in 10 unknown), comprehension drops significantly, and guessing from context becomes statistically random
facts.note: Mostly Verified. 90% cited as minimum threshold; 'statistically random' slightly overstated but directionally correct.

facts.fact: Reading comprehension is highly correlated with background knowledge and domain-specific vocabulary
facts.note: Verified. Hirsch's core thesis, widely supported in reading research.

facts.fact: 'General' reading strategies do not compensate for a lack of vocabulary
facts.note: Verified. Central to Hirsch's argument against 'skills-based' reading instruction.

facts.fact: A balanced course requires: Meaning-Focused Input, Meaning-Focused Output, Language-Focused Learning, and Fluency Development
facts.note: Verified. Nation's Four Strands framework.

facts.fact: 'Language-Focused Learning' (drills) should only be ~25% of the time but is the most efficient for initial acquisition
facts.note: Mostly Verified. 25% is correct (four strands). 'Most efficient' claim is supported but nuanced.
